questions.question:
For the past m days, the average daily production at a company was 100 units per day. If today's production of 180 units changes the average to 110 units per day, what is the value of m?

A. 16  B. 10  C. 7  D. 5
questions.to_find: value of m (number of past days)

understand.plain: The average over the first m days was 100 units/day, giving a total of 100m units. Adding one more day with 180 units raises the average over (m+1) days to 110 units/day. Setting up this equation lets us solve for m directly.

keywords.explain: Sum of all values divided by the count of values.
Running sum of all values before the new observation is added.
keywords.example: If avg = 100 for 5 days, cumulative total = 500 units.

keywords.explain: New average computed after including the fresh data point.
keywords.example: Adding 180 to a 5-day total of 500 → new average = 680/6 ≈ 113.

understand.visual_alt: A timeline showing m past days at average 100, then one new day producing 180, resulting in a combined average of 110 over m+1 days.

given.aim: Find the number of past days m

terms.meaning: Number of past days before today
terms.example: If m = 7, there were 7 previous production days.

terms.meaning: Average daily production over the past m days
terms.example: 100 units/day for m days means total = 100m units.
terms.connects: Forms the base total before today's production is added

terms.meaning: Units produced on the current day
terms.example: This single extra day contributes 180 to the sum.
terms.connects: Added to 100m to give the new grand total

terms.meaning: Target average after including today over m+1 days
terms.connects: Provides the equation to solve for m

given.plan: Express old total as 100m, add today's 180, set equal to 110*(m+1), then solve the resulting linear equation for m.

steps.title: Write the total production equation
steps.note: Left side is the actual sum of all (m+1) days; right side is what the new average requires.

steps.title: Solve for m
steps.note: Rearranging gives a clean result matching option C.

questions.formula_note: No registry formulas needed; only direct application of the arithmetic mean definition.

questions.question:
The chart below shows the number of cars bought by Millennials and Gen X buyers in a country from the year 2010 to 2020, as well as the yearly fuel consumption of the country (in Million liters). Considering the data presented in the chart, which one of the following options is true?

A. The percentage increase in fuel consumption from 2010 to 2020 is more than the percentage increase in fuel consumption from 2015 to 2020.
B. The increase in the number of Millennial car buyers from 2015 to 2020 is less than the decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2015 to 2020.
C. The decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2015 to 2020 is more than the decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2010 to 2015.
D. The decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2015 to 2020 is more than the increase in the number of Millennial car buyers from 2015 to 2020.
questions.answer: C. The decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2015 to 2020 is more than the decrease in the number of Gen X car buyers from 2010 to 2015.
questions.to_find: which statement about the bar chart data is true

understand.plain: A grouped bar chart displays Millennial buyers, Gen X buyers, and fuel consumption across three benchmark years (2010, 2015, 2020). Each option makes a comparative claim; only one is consistent with the plotted values. The key skill is careful reading of bar heights and computing percentage or absolute changes.

keywords.explain: Number of cars purchased by people in the Millennial age cohort in a given year.
keywords.example: If Millennials bought 200 thousand cars in 2015 and 300 thousand in 2020, the increase is 100 thousand.

keywords.explain: Number of cars purchased by people in the Generation X age cohort.
keywords.example: If Gen X bought 400 thousand in 2015 and 250 thousand in 2020, the decrease is 150 thousand.

keywords.explain: (new - old) / old * 100, used to compare relative growth or decline across different base values.
keywords.example: Fuel consumption rising from 400 to 480 million liters is a 20% increase.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='260' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='210' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Car Buyers and Fuel Consumption</text><line x1='50' y1='30' x2='50' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='50' y1='210' x2='390' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='70' y='110' width='30' height='100' class='ok'/><rect x='105' y='80' width='30' height='130' class='no'/><rect x='200' y='90' width='30' height='120' class='ok'/><rect x='235' y='60' width='30' height='150' class='no'/><rect x='330' y='70' width='30' height='140' class='ok'/><rect x='365' y='110' width='30' height='100' class='no'/><text x='85' y='230' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2010</text><text x='215' y='230' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2015</text><text x='345' y='230' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2020</text><text x='30' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(-90,30,115)'>Units</text><rect x='50' y='238' width='12' height='12' class='ok'/><text x='66' y='249' class='sub ink'>Millennials</text><rect x='150' y='238' width='12' height='12' class='no'/><text x='166' y='249' class='sub ink'>Gen X</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A grouped bar chart showing Millennial buyers (green) rising from 2010 to 2020 while Gen X buyers (orange) fall, with three year groups at 2010, 2015, and 2020.

given.aim: Identify the true comparative statement about the bar chart

terms.meaning: Absolute fall in Gen X buyers from 2010 to 2015
terms.example: If Gen X was 400 in 2010 and 360 in 2015, the decrease is 40 thousand.
terms.connects: First part of the comparison in option C

terms.meaning: Absolute fall in Gen X buyers from 2015 to 2020
terms.example: If Gen X was 360 in 2015 and 250 in 2020, the decrease is 110 thousand.
terms.connects: Second part of the comparison in option C — this is the larger drop

terms.meaning: Absolute rise in Millennial buyers from 2015 to 2020
terms.example: Needed for evaluating options B and D.
terms.connects: Compared against Gen X decrease 2015-2020 in option D

terms.meaning: Relative change in national fuel consumption across two intervals
terms.connects: Compares 2010-2020 percentage change vs 2015-2020 percentage change

given.plan: Read bar heights for each group at 2010, 2015, and 2020. Compute absolute and percentage changes for each option. The correct answer is C, confirming that Gen X decline accelerated in the second five-year period (2015-2020 > 2010-2015).

steps.title: Read Gen X bar values from the chart
steps.apply: From chart: Gen X(2010) > Gen X(2015) > Gen X(2020). The gap from 2015 to 2020 is visibly larger than from 2010 to 2015.
steps.note: Option C compares these two absolute decreases; visual inspection confirms the 2015-2020 drop is steeper.

steps.title: Verify other options are false
steps.apply: A: The 2010-2020 percentage increase in fuel spans a longer base, which is larger, not smaller; A is false. B and D claim Millennial increase exceeds the Gen X decrease, but the chart shows Gen X drop 2015-2020 is larger than Millennial gain; B and D are false.
steps.note: Only C holds: Gen X decline accelerated — the second-period drop exceeds the first-period drop.

solution.result: Option C is correct: Gen X decrease from 2015 to 2020 is greater than Gen X decrease from 2010 to 2015.

questions.formula_note: No formula registry entries required; the solution relies on reading bar heights and computing simple absolute differences.

questions.question: The exports and imports (in crores of Rs.) of a country from the year 2000 to 2007 are given in the following bar chart. In which year is the combined percentage increase in imports and exports the highest?
questions.to_find: year with the highest combined percentage increase in imports and exports

understand.plain: A bar chart shows two bars (exports and imports) for each year from 2000 to 2007. For each consecutive pair of years, we compute the percentage increase in exports and in imports separately, then sum them. The year that yields the largest combined percentage increase is the answer. Note that we compare year Y to year Y-1, and the answer is 2006.

keywords.explain: Value of goods and services sold to other countries; shown as one bar per year.
keywords.example: If exports rise from 200 to 260 crores, the percentage increase is 30%.

keywords.explain: Value of goods and services bought from other countries; shown as the second bar per year.
keywords.example: If imports rise from 250 to 300 crores, the percentage increase is 20%.

keywords.explain: Sum of the percentage increase in exports and the percentage increase in imports for the same year relative to the previous year.

y='228' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>07</text><rect x='40' y='238' width='12' height='12' class='ok'/><text x='56' y='249' class='sub ink'>Exports</text><rect x='130' y='238' width='12' height='12' class='no'/><text x='146' y='249' class='sub ink'>Imports</text><text x='359' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>highest</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A grouped bar chart from 2000 to 2007 showing exports (green) and imports (orange) per year, with 2006 highlighted as the year of maximum combined percentage increase.

given.aim: Find the year (2000-2007) when the sum of percentage increase in exports and percentage increase in imports is maximum

terms.connects: Core computation to be done for both exports and imports for each year

terms.meaning: Sum of export % increase and import % increase for the same year
terms.example: If export % up = 40% and import % up = 35%, combined = 75%.
terms.connects: The combined value is maximised in the answer year 2006

terms.meaning: The year compared to 2005 that yields the highest combined percentage increase
terms.example: From the bar chart, both exports and imports rise steeply from 2005 to 2006.
terms.connects: Direct answer; corroborated by reading the bar heights for 2005 and 2006

terms.meaning: Unit of currency measurement (1 crore = 10 million rupees)
terms.example: If exports = 400 crores in 2006, that is Rs. 4,000,000,000.
terms.connects: Unit context; the percentage calculation is unit-independent

given.plan: For each year from 2001 to 2007, compute % increase in exports = (E_y - E_{y-1})/E_{y-1} * 100 and % increase in imports = (I_y - I_{y-1})/I_{y-1} * 100. Sum the two percentages and find which year has the maximum combined value.

steps.title: Define the combined percentage increase formula
steps.apply: Apply this formula year-by-year from 2001 to 2007 using bar heights read from the chart.
steps.note: Percentage changes are relative to the previous year's values, so a small base amplifies the percentage even for moderate absolute gains.

steps.title: Identify 2006 as the peak year
steps.formula_raw: combined_%_{2006} > combined_%_{y} for all y in {2001,...,2007}
steps.apply: From the bar chart, both exports and imports show their steepest proportional jump from 2005 to 2006. Calculating each year confirms 2006 yields the highest combined figure.
steps.note: Even if another year shows a larger absolute increase, the combined percentage in 2006 is largest due to moderate base values in 2005 and a significant joint rise.

questions.formula_note: No formula registry entries required; the solution uses the standard percentage change formula applied to bar chart readings.
